Reinforcement Learning (RL) is linked with language in the context of language-conditioned RL and language-assisted RL, where language is used to condition the problem formulation and assist the agent in learning.
Language-Conditioned RL (LC-RL) is a subset of Reinforcement Learning (RL) where language conditions the formulation of the problem.
Language-Conditioned RL (LC-RL) specifically focuses on using language instructions to optimize the corresponding reward function.
The reward function in LC-RL is sparse, which leads to sample inefficiency in algorithms.
To tackle reward sparsity, one approach is to densify the reward by decomposing the general goal into sub-goals and rewarding them individually.

The sum of all the intrinsic rewards measures the quality of a trajectory in relation to the given goal.
In other words, maximizing this intrinsic reward incentivizes the agent to produce behaviour that unambiguously explains various aspects of the given goal.

The main difficulty in ranking trajectories is that many trajectories can share the same description.
Many trajectories can share the same description and thus can answer questions correctly.
A human assessing several trajectories, with the same extrinsic reward, relatively to the same goal would prioritize the simplest one.
To account for this, we make the reward proportional to the confidence in the answer at time step t.
An overly complex trajectory being harder to understand for the QA, the answer should be given with less confidence.

Training trajectories are used to train the QA model by providing full trajectory as input and questions generated by the QG. The cross-entropy loss over the output distribution is used to update the QA.

QA is trained to answer 'no_answer' by preventing random guessing. Some trajectories and questions from unrelated goals are associated to ensure the QA learns to pay attention to details and not guess the answer by chance.
Trajectory from a specific goal is associated with a question generated from a different goal to train the QA to understand and answer correctly.

The correct answer to certain questions, such as when the trajectory describes an action different from the question, is 'no_answer'. This ensures the QA model learns to pay attention to details and not provide incorrect answers.

QA performs more efficiently when learning from a broad distribution of trajectories for similar tasks. Learning from a narrow distribution may result in the QA model not recognizing noisy trajectories during training, leading to frequent 'no_answer' responses.
